quotes.text: Nobody remembers who came second, sweetheart.
quotes.speaker: All About Eve (1950)
quotes.context: Margo Channing, to a younger actress backstage
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the sharp, theatrical bite and the 'sweetheart' with a blade inside it are pitch-perfect for Margo, and the competitive-fame theme is central to the film. The subtle giveaway is that it's a fraction too pithy; Margo's real barbs tend to unfurl over a few more words.

quotes.text: I never said I was the good guy, Colonel. I said I was the necessary one.
quotes.speaker: Apocalypse Now (1979)
quotes.context: Kurtz, speaking quietly to Willard in the compound
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Kurtz's philosophical, self-justifying monologues are exactly this rhythm, and the 'necessary' framing echoes real lines about order and chaos in the film. The subtle giveaway is the direct address to rank; Kurtz tends to speak in more mythic, impersonal terms rather than military titles.

quotes.text: Frankly, my dear, I don't give a damn.
quotes.speaker: Gone with the Wind (1939)
quotes.context: Rhett Butler's (Clark Gable) parting words to Scarlett O'Hara as he leaves her at the end of the film

quotes.speaker: Finding Nemo (2003)

quotes.text: My mama always said life was like a box of chocolates. You never know what you're gonna get.
quotes.speaker: Forrest Gump (1994)
quotes.context: Forrest (Tom Hanks) sits on a bus-stop bench recounting his mother's favorite saying.

quotes.text: I used to think silence meant peace. Then I learned what peace actually costs.
quotes.speaker: Platoon (1986)
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Taylor's voiceover narration throughout the film has exactly this reflective, disillusioned cadence, drawing on his letters home. The subtle giveaway is that it's slightly too clean and resolved; Oliver Stone's real narration lines tend to be rougher and more searching.
